title: Macros Are Just Register Contents
subtitle: {key:"ap} → edit → {key:"ay$} — the macro lifecycle as text editing.
summary: A recorded macro is text in a register. Paste it, edit it, yank it back. This unification of macros and clipboard text is one of Vim's smartest design choices.

blocks.text: {key:q}{key:a}…{key:q} records a macro into register `a`. {key:@}{key:a} plays it. But the register `a` is the **same `a`** you yank text into — so the macro is just text. Which means you can edit it like any other text.

A typical refinement loop looks like:

  1. Record a draft: {key:q}{key:a}…{key:q}.
  2. It mostly works but has a glitch.
  3. {key:o} (open a new line), then {key:"}{key:a}{key:p} — the keystrokes appear as text in the buffer.
  4. Edit them. Maybe `dawi` should have been `daWi`. Replace.
  5. {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:$} (yank from cursor to end of line back into register `a`) or {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:y} for the whole line.
  6. {key:u} to undo the buffer changes (you don't want the keystroke text staying in your file).
  7. {key:@}{key:a} — try the macro again with the fix.

blocks.label: Paste the macro into the buffer
Yank from cursor to EOL back into a

blocks.title: Why This Works
blocks.text:
Recording a macro **just** appends each typed key to the register, encoded as a Vim keystroke string ({key:<Esc>} is one byte 0x1b, etc.). Playback is a function `feedkeys(getreg('a'))` — feed those keystrokes back into the input buffer.

Nothing about a macro is "compiled." There's no AST, no special structure. So:

  • Yank works on macros: {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}{key:$} writes whatever's selected.
  • Append works on macros: {key:q}{key:A}…{key:q} appends to register `a`'s existing contents — extending the macro.
  • `:let` {key:@}{key:a}{key:=}{string} sets the macro programmatically.
  • You can yank a macro from a comment in your file by selecting the keystroke text and {key:"}{key:a}{key:y}.

Special keys appear as {key:<Esc>}, {key:<CR>}, etc. in normal display, but as raw bytes in the register. Use `:let` {key:@}{key:a}{key:=}"ciw foo<Esc>" to type-set special keys (the {key:<Esc>} is interpreted by Vim's parser).

When you {key:"}{key:a}{key:p}-paste a macro and special keys show as `^[` or similar weirdness, that's the literal escape character. Most terminals will display it. The macro still works — it's just *not* meant to be human-readable in the buffer.
